ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for deep-fried squid paste ball; closest defensible match is 'squid food product' (FOODON_03315595). Processed ball form not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers squid and cuttlefish products broadly; no specific code for deep-fried squid paste balls exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sotong ball or squid paste ball as a distinct food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of sotong balls consists of 4 balls on a skewer, weighing approximately 80 g, often served with chili sauce or sweet sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Sotong Ball — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sotong Ball (F03040013). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sotong ball in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) by analogy with processed fish ball / surimi-type products with starch binders, which have GI values in the 45–55 range per Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed data. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (80 g × 12.8 g/100 g = 10.24 g) / 100 = 5.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 729 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g hypertension hard gate
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sotong balls to no more than 1–2 pieces per occasion, as a single 80 g serving delivers approximately 583 mg sodium; avoid pairing with high-sodium dipping sauces such as chili or soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 12.8 g/100 g with low sugar (2.0 g) and moderate fibre (2.1 g); deep-fried preparation adds fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sotong balls have a low estimated GI (~52) and moderate carbohydrate content; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a small portion (3–4 balls) as part of a balanced meal, but should account for the carbohydrate load and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (729 mg/100 g) and deep-fried fat content (9.6 g/100 g) with ultra-processed formulation including MSG and preservatives
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid sotong balls due to the combination of high sodium and deep-fried fat, both of which contribute to elevated blood pressure and atherogenic risk; if consumed, restrict to 1–2 balls and avoid additional salty condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 729 mg/100 g exceeds 400 mg/100 g RA threshold; deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sotong balls as the high sodium content worsens tissue swelling and the deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sotong ball (鱿鱼丸; Bebola sotong) is a popular Singaporean hawker snack made from minced squid paste mixed with starch, salt, MSG, and seasonings, shaped into balls and deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining chewy and springy inside. The name 'sotong' is the Malay word for squid or cuttlefish, widely used in Singapore and Malaysia. Sotong balls are commonly sold at hawker centres, food courts, and pasar malam (night markets), typically skewered on bamboo sticks and served with sweet chili sauce or sambal. Nutritionally, per 100 g they provide 184 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 9.6 g fat, and 12.8 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 729 mg — a consequence of the heavy seasoning and preservatives used in commercial production. The dietary fibre content of 2.1 g/100 g is moderate for a processed seafood product. Clinically, the high sodium level (729 mg/100 g) makes sotong balls unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and the deep-fried preparation adds atherogenic fat. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the estimated low GI (~52) and moderate carbohydrate content make occasional small portions acceptable within a controlled diet. The ultra-processed nature of this product, with added MSG and preservatives, means it should be consumed in moderation by the general population.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sotong ball?
Is sotong ball high in sodium?
What is sotong ball made of?
Is sotong ball suitable for hypertension?
Can I eat sotong ball if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03040013) with confidence 0.95; this is the canonical approved primary source for Singaporean food records. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed University of Sydney GI Database data for similar processed fish/surimi ball products.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for sotong ball are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03040013 as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary data available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific processed squid ball product. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on processed fish ball / surimi-type products from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for sotong ball exists in approved sources. Halal status assumed based on typical commercial production in Singapore using halal-certified squid and ingredients; individual product verification recommended.
